content:
# 2 Timothy Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TI_003_page_001.jpg

1. But understand this, that in the last days there will come times of difficulty.

2. For people will be lovers of self, lovers of money, proud, arrogant, abusive, disobedient to their parents, ungrateful, unholy,

3. heartless, unappeasable, slanderous, without self-control, brutal, not loving good,

4. treacherous, reckless, swollen with conceit, lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God,

5. having the appearance of godliness, but denying its power. Avoid such people.

6. For among them are those who creep into households and capture weak women, burdened with sins and led astray by various passions,

7. always learning and never able to arrive at a knowledge of the truth.

8. Just as Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses, so these men also oppose the truth, men corrupted in mind and disqualified regarding the faith.

9. But they will not get very far, for their folly will be plain to all, as was that of those two men.

10. You, however, have followed my teaching, my conduct, my aim in life, my faith, my patience, my love, my steadfastness,

11. my persecutions and sufferings that happened to me at Antioch, at Iconium, and at Lystra — which persecutions I endured; yet from them all the Lord rescued me.

12. Indeed, all who desire to live a godly life in Christ Jesus will be persecuted,

13. while evil people and impostors will go on from bad to worse, deceiving and being deceived.

14. But as for you, continue in what you have learned and have firmly believed, knowing from whom you learned it

15. and how from childhood you have been acquainted with the sacred writings, which are able to make you wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus.

16. All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness,

17. that the man of God may be complete, equipped for every good work.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 3 moves from warning (the vice list, vv.2-5) to example (Paul's own suffering, vv.10-12) to foundation (Scripture, vv.14-17). The chapter's theological climax is v.16 — theopneustos, "God-breathed" — the most concise statement on the nature of Scripture in the entire New Testament.

**Verses 2-5 — The vice list:** All three translators captured the nineteen terms. SOPHIA affirms: the list is deliberately bookended by philautoi ("lovers of self") and philotheoi ("lovers of God") — self-love displaces God-love.

**Verse 16 — theopneustos:** EMET: "God-breathed." LOGOS: "breathed out by God." PNEUMA: "inspired by God." All capture the concept; SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's "breathed out by God" as the most precise English rendering of the Greek compound.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.5 — "having denied its power" vs. "denying its power" vs. "reject the power that could make them godly"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having a form of godliness but having denied its power" | morphōsin eusebeias tēn de dynamin autēs ērnēmenoi |
| LOGOS | "having the appearance of godliness, but denying its power" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "They will act religious, but they will reject the power that could make them godly" | "Act religious" for morphōsin eusebeias; adds "that could make them godly" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. morphōsis eusebeias means "form/appearance of godliness" — they have the outward shape but not the inner reality. PNEUMA's "act religious" captures the sense but "the power that could make them godly" adds a purpose clause not in the Greek. Paul says they deny "its power" — the power of godliness itself — without specifying what that power would accomplish.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds purpose clause.

---

### v.6 — "weak women" vs. "vulnerable women" vs. "win the confidence of vulnerable women"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "captivating weak women loaded down with sins" | gynaikaria — diminutive |
| LOGOS | "capture weak women, burdened with sins" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "win the confidence of vulnerable women who are burdened with the guilt of sin" | "Win the confidence of" for aichmalōtizontes; "guilt of sin" for hamartiais |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. aichmalōtizō means "to take captive" — a military metaphor. PNEUMA's "win the confidence of" softens the image: Paul's point is that these teachers capture (like prisoners of war), not that they build trust. PNEUMA also adds "guilt of" — Paul says burdened with sins, not with guilt about sins.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA softens the captivity metaphor and adds "guilt."

---

### v.8 — "worthless concerning the faith" vs. "disqualified regarding the faith" vs. "a counterfeit faith"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "worthless concerning the faith" | adokimoi peri tēn pistin |
| LOGOS | "disqualified regarding the faith" | adokimoi — "not standing the test" |
| PNEUMA | "They have depraved minds and a counterfeit faith" | "Counterfeit faith" — shifts from "failed test" to "fake" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. adokimos means "not standing the test / disqualified / rejected upon examination." It describes people who have been tested and found wanting — not that their faith is counterfeit (an imitation), but that they have been disqualified with respect to the faith. PNEUMA's "counterfeit" shifts the metaphor from testing to forgery.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes metaphor from testing to counterfeiting.

---

### v.14 — "knowing from whom you learned" — singular or plural?

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "knowing from whom you learned them" | para tinōn — plural |
| LOGOS | "knowing from whom you learned it" | "Whom" — could be singular or plural |
| PNEUMA | "you know you can trust those who taught you" | "Those who" — explicitly plural; adds "trust" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek para tinōn is plural ("from whom" = multiple teachers), referring to Paul, Lois, Eunice, and other teachers. LOGOS preserves the ambiguity. PNEUMA explicitly pluralizes and adds "trust" — Paul says "knowing from whom," not "knowing you can trust those who."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "trust."

---

### v.16 — "God-breathed" vs. "breathed out by God" vs. "inspired by God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "All scripture is God-breathed" | theopneustos — compound adjective |
| LOGOS | "All Scripture is breathed out by God" | Expands compound into phrase |
| PNEUMA | "All Scripture is inspired by God" | "Inspired" — Latin-derived, traditional |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. theopneustos is a compound adjective: theos (God) + pneō (breathe). "Inspired" (Latin: inspirare, "to breathe into") is the traditional English rendering but suggests God breathing into the text, whereas the Greek emphasizes that Scripture is breathed out from God — its origin, not its subsequent vivification. "Breathed out by God" better captures the direction of the metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Inspired" reverses the direction of the breathing metaphor.

---

### v.17 — "the man of God" vs. "his people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that the man of God may be complete, fully equipped" | ho tou theou anthrōpos |
| LOGOS | "that the man of God may be complete, equipped" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "God uses it to prepare and equip his people to do every good work" | "His people" for ho anthrōpos tou theou; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. ho tou theou anthrōpos is singular: "the man of God" — a title with Old Testament roots (Moses, Elijah, Timothy). PNEUMA pluralizes to "his people" and restructures the sentence, making God the subject rather than Scripture. Paul's point is that Scripture's purpose is to make the man of God complete.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA pluralizes and restructures.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 3 | "That could make them godly" (v.5), "guilt of" (v.6), "trust" (v.14) |
| PNEUMA softens/shifts metaphors | 2 | "Win the confidence of" for captivity (v.6), "counterfeit" for disqualified (v.8) |
| PNEUMA restructures | 2 | God as subject instead of Scripture (v.17), "inspired" reverses breathing direction (v.16) |
| PNEUMA pluralizes | 1 | "His people" for "the man of God" (v.17) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 3's most theologically significant disagreement is v.16 — the rendering of theopneustos. "Inspired" (PNEUMA) comes from Latin inspirare ("to breathe into"), suggesting God breathed life into pre-existing texts. But the Greek compound means "breathed out by God" — Scripture originates from God's breath. The direction of the metaphor matters: outbreathing (origin) vs. inbreathing (vivification). PNEUMA also pluralizes "the man of God" to "his people" (v.17), losing the singular Hebraic title, and softens the captivity metaphor in v.6.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
